subject: [RFC PATCH v2 0/7] mm, swap: Virtual Swap Space (Swap Table Edition)

reviews.author: YoungJun Park
reviews.summary: Reviewer YoungJun Park noted that the current interface for vswap is a better fit than using a per-tier max, but acknowledged that this might lead to similar usage patterns if only 0 or max can be set.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Nhat,

Tier 1: VSWAP, Tier 2: ZSWAP ...

I don't see any problem applying the desired functionality with the
currently proposed mechanism and interface. With this, a user would be
assigned the default Virtual -> RAM swap tier, and the overall picture
becomes one where swap tiers are composed according to the priority
setting.

A few more thoughts came to mind.

Shakeel also proposed a per-tier max for the swap tier interface.

https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/aiw2p5ANjsQUCIHA@linux.dev/

However, for vswap, rather than treating it as a case for limiting the
amount via such a per-tier max, I think the current interface is the
better fit. (But, as Shakeel mentioned, if we only allow the limit
to be set to 0 or max, the usage could end up being the same. I'm still
thinking this part through.)

I have a few other thoughts as well, but I plan to raise those points in
the swap tier discussion thread instead. Please take a look at the
related thread, and let me know if you have any opinions. :)

And I'll share more if other thoughts come to mind

Thanks,
Youngjun Park

reviews.author: Nhat Pham (author)
reviews.summary:
Author is seeking feedback on whether vswap should be treated as a tier, currently abstract and transparent to userspace, but potentially complex if exposed as a separate device.

Author is still considering the feedback from YoungJun Park, but has not yet formed an opinion or provided any specific response to address the concern.
sentiment_signals: author is still thinking about it
reviews.raw_body:
It's more - is there a strong argument to let vswap be a tier (which
is not supported by just turning of vswap altogether).

Because right now I'm not exposing vswap device to userspace in any
manner, pretty much. It's abstract and transparent, and minimizes
complexity (no vswap and swap.tier interaction) and surfaces for
issues.

But if you have a strong use case in mind please let me know :)

Worst case scenario if we're wrong, we can always do it as a follow-up
down the line.

---

I'm following that thread too. I'm still thinking about it - will let
you know when I have a more definitive opinion.

2026-06-14.patch_summary: This patch series introduces Virtual Swap Space (Swap Table Edition), which aims to address the limitations of current swap design by decoupling swap entries from physical backing slots. This allows for more efficient use of disk space, especially in scenarios where zswap is used. The changes include rebasing to a newer mm-unstable tip, fixing assorted issues, and simplifying code. Additionally, the patch series introduces new features such as deferring memcg_table allocation on physical clusters until the first vswap-backing slot installs and widening swap_info_struct->max and ->pages to support up to 8 PB of swap space.

reviews.author: Yosry Ahmed
reviews.summary: Reviewer Yosry Ahmed expressed concerns that vswap entries can be backed by either zswap or an actual swap device, creating a tiering issue, and suggested that vswap should not be exposed to userspace at this time.
reviews.raw_body:
I definitely think vswap should *not* be a tier. First of all, a vswap
entry can be backed by zswap or an actual swap device, which would be
two different tiers. How does that work?

I also think vswap should not be exposed to userspace in any way, at
least not now. I still think we should aim to just make the
redirection layer always on and eliminate "vswap devices".

2026-06-15.patch_summary: This patch series introduces Virtual Swap Space (Swap Table Edition), which aims to address the limitations of current swap design by decoupling swap entries from physical backing slots. This allows for more efficient use of disk space, especially in scenarios where zswap is used. The changes include rebasing to a newer mm-unstable tip, fixing assorted issues, and simplifying code. Additionally, the patch series introduces new features such as deferring memcg_table allocation on physical clusters until the first vswap-backing slot installs and widening swap_info_struct->max and ->pages to support up to 8 PB of swap space.

reviews.author: YoungJun Park
reviews.summary: Reviewer YoungJun Park agrees with keeping vswap user-transparent but suggests disabling it entirely if necessary, relying on CONFIG_VSWAP, and revisiting the design in the future if a strong use case for user interaction emerges.
reviews.raw_body:
After following the answers and giving it some thought, I agree that
vswap should be kept user-transparent. If there is a strict need to
disable it, relying on CONFIG_VSWAP to remove it entirely seems like
the right approach.

If a strong use case for user interaction emerges in the future, we can
revisit the design and figure out how to handle it at that time.

Thanks,
Youngjun Park

reviews.author: Nhat Pham (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that per-cgroup vswapping might be necessary but only if certain conditions are met, and plans to stick with transparent vswap for the next version.
reviews.raw_body:
Yeah the only argument for adding vswap to swap tier is if we want it
to virtualize swap on a per-cgroup basis, assuming:

1. There's a setup where some cgroups benefit from vswap and some
don't, in the same deployment or host (so you can't just use
CONFIG_VSWAP).

2. We can't decide it with some heuristics purely based on kernel's
knowledge (so for e.g, if a cgroup enables zswap, then vswap probably
makes more sense than not, etc. etc.).

Maybe I'm missing something, and if so please let me know. But
otherwise I'll stick with transparent vswap for the next version.

With Youngjun's new interface, if we made a mistake here and
per-cgroup vswapping turned out to be necessary, fixing it is fairly
cheap. We don't need to add any new knob - just need to expose it to
memory.swap.tier somehow, and we're done. That can be done as
follow-up :)

2026-06-16.patch_summary: This patch series introduces Virtual Swap Space (Swap Table Edition), which aims to address the limitations of current swap design by decoupling swap entries from physical backing slots. This allows for more efficient use of disk space, especially in scenarios where zswap is used. The changes include rebasing to a newer mm-unstable tip, fixing assorted issues, and simplifying code. Additionally, the patch series introduces new features such as deferring memcg_table allocation on physical clusters until the first vswap-backing slot installs and widening swap_info_struct->max and ->pages to support up to 8 PB of swap space.

reviews.author: Yosry Ahmed
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that a comment intended for an earlier patch was mistakenly included in this one, and that another comment's changes were being undone.

Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that the code should only execute in the !ret case, but this is not immediately clear from the current implementation. They suggested moving the check to if (!ret) and also proposed alternative reordering of the code to avoid indentation issues.

Reviewer Yosry Ahmed suggested avoiding THP swapin for vswap initially, noting that it's not specific to vswap and could be allowed even without vswap if an entire folio is on-disk.

Reviewer Yosry Ahmed questioned whether calling folio_release_vswap_backing() is necessary, suggesting that __vswap_release_backing() directly calls zswap_entry_free() and no vswap-specific state is cleaned up.

reviews.author: Yosry Ahmed
reviews.summary: Reviewer Yosry Ahmed questioned whether calling `folio_realloc_swap()` without first loading the swap entry is a valid use case and suggested modifying the API to take the swap entry directly, allowing for the load to be done within.
reviews.raw_body:
I didn't look through the rest of the series, but are there use cases
for calling folio_realloc_swap() without calling vswap_zswap_load()
first? I wonder if the realloc_swap API should take the swpentry
directly and do the load within? Something like
vswap_alloc_phys(swpentry, folio)?

reviews.author: Nhat Pham (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that the swapout fallback path also uses phys swap space allocation and agreed to avoid calling zswap_load() in this case.
reviews.raw_body:
It's also use on the swapout fallback path! If zswap rejects the page
or is disabled, and memory.zswap.writeback=1, then we allocate phys
swap space. We probably don't wanna do zswap_load() there again :)

reviews.author: Yosry Ahmed
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Yosry Ahmed suggested abstracting xarray operations and vswap operations into separate helpers, such as zswap_tree_store() and zswap_tree_load(), to reduce code divergence and improve maintainability.

Reviewer Yosry Ahmed suggested abstracting away the xarray vs. vswap distinction in the code by introducing a helper function that can remove zswap entries from vswap, and also proposed creating additional helpers to simplify the code.
reviews.raw_body:
There's a lot of divergence in the code (in this patch and previous
ones). Seems like a lot of it is to do xarray operations vs vswap
operations. I wonder if we can abstract these into helpers, e.g.
zswap_tree_store(), zswap_tree_load(), etc. Maybe the name is not the
best, but you get the point :)

Here we can then do zswap_tree_load() for both code paths and only the
folio_realloc_swap() needs to be different for vswap. We can do
similar cleanups for the load/store paths as well.

---

Maybe this can also be abstracted into a helper, but I wonder what the
corresponding vswap operation would be. I think folio_realloc_swap()
will have already "erased" the zswap entry from vswap. Maybe have a
vswap helper that will only remove it if it's a zswap entry? We can
probably do a lockless check first to make it cheap?

It's probably silly to do this, and maybe there's a better way.
Generally, I think the code would be easier to follow if we abstract
away the xarray vs. vswap stuff into helpers (where it's reasonable).

2026-06-23.patch_summary: This patch series introduces Virtual Swap Space (Swap Table Edition), which aims to address the limitations of current swap design by decoupling swap entries from physical backing slots. This allows for more efficient use of disk space, especially in scenarios where zswap is used. The changes include rebasing to a newer mm-unstable tip, fixing assorted issues, and simplifying code. Additionally, the patch series introduces new features such as deferring memcg_table allocation on physical clusters until the first vswap-backing slot installs and widening swap_info_struct->max and ->pages to support up to 8 PB of swap space.
